translation: Indeed, We sent the Qur'an down during the Night of Decree
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ

Allah ﷻ begins this remarkable surah by declaring: "Indeed, We sent it down in the Night of Power." The opening carries profound weight in how Allah ﷻ speaks of the revelation. Notice that He says "We sent it down" rather than "We sent Our angel down to you." By removing Jibreel (AS) from this equation, Allah ﷻ takes direct credit for the revelation, making it even more personal and direct. The words are His own, sent down specifically in this blessed night.

The pronoun "it" (هُ) in أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ is especially significant. This is what Arabic linguists call a pronoun of familiarity. When you say "Did you watch it?" or "Did you see it?" to someone, they either have no idea what you're talking about, or both of you are thinking of the same thing. When Allah ﷻ says "We sent it down," He's indicating that the Quran had already become such a phenomenon in Makkah that it was on everyone's mind. When people heard "it," they couldn't think of anything other than the Quran. Even the enemies of Islam knew exactly what "it" referred to. The Quran had permeated and flooded the city of Makkah so completely that it didn't even need to be spelled out by name. This is the power and grandeur of the Quran captured in a single pronoun.

The phrase إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ("Indeed, We sent it down") has been understood by scholars in different ways. Ibn Abbas (RA) held a view that many scholars believe he learned directly from the Prophet ﷺ himself: that Allah ﷻ sent the entire Quran from the seventh heaven to the first heaven in one night—this Night of Power. Then, from that place in the first heaven, it was revealed gradually to the Prophet ﷺ over twenty-three years. The complete revelation came down in one night, and then the detailed, gradual revelation unfolded over the Prophet's mission.

Scholars are inclined to believe Ibn Abbas (RA) learned this from the Messenger ﷺ himself because this is a matter of the unseen. A companion cannot speak with confidence about what happens in the seventh heaven or the first heaven unless he heard it from the one who was informed about the unseen—the Prophet ﷺ. While Ibn Abbas (RA) doesn't quote the Prophet ﷺ directly, scholars consider this knowledge to be traced back to him.

Another interpretation is that "We sent it down" means "We began sending it down"—that the process of revelation to the Prophet ﷺ started in this night. The pronoun could also refer back to the previous surah, Surah Al-Alaq, connecting that first revelation with this Night of Power.

Some have suggested that Laylat al-Qadr refers to the middle of Sha'ban, citing narrations from Aisha (RA) about the blessings of that month. However, this view is decisively rejected because Allah ﷻ explicitly states in Surah Al-Baqarah: "The month of Ramadan is that in which the Quran was sent down" (2:185). Every night of Ramadan belongs within the revelation month, and this night must therefore be in Ramadan. The connection between the Quran's revelation and Ramadan is ironclad.

Now let's explore three essential meanings of the word قَدْر  that frame our understanding of this surah.

**First meaning: Decree and Calculation**

One meaning of qadr in Arabic is calculation or measure—to set limits or determine the extent of something. Allah ﷻ uses this meaning when He says, "We calculated the stages for the moon" (قَدَّرَهُ مَنَازِلَ). Applied to Laylat al-Qadr, this is the night when Allah ﷻ sends down decisions—major decisions for the coming year. All the good decisions for the rest of the year are determined: who will earn what, who will experience what happiness, what child will be born, what marriage will take place, who will come to Islam, who will die a peaceful death. These matters are already known by Allah ﷻ, but the angels come to begin executing these decisions in this night.

Think of it like a government budget that gets approved and then executed. This is that night—when the heavenly decree made by Allah ﷻ is sent down and the decisions begin to be carried out by the angels.

This understanding is confirmed by what Allah ﷻ says in Surah Ad-Dukhan: "By the clear Book, indeed We sent it down during a blessed night—indeed, We were to warn. In it every precise matter is made distinct, as a command from Us. Indeed, We were to send a messenger, as mercy from your Lord" (44:2-6). Allah ﷻ describes this as the night when every wise decision is separated and distinguished—the night of great decision-making. These commands come from Allah ﷻ as mercy, not as calamity or destruction. They are loving commands filled with care.

And critically, Allah ﷻ ends that passage: "Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing" (44:6). He hears what's going on inside your heart and the supplications you're making, especially in this night.

**Second meaning: Honor and Nobility**

Another meaning of qadr is rank, nobility, or honor (مَنزِلَة or شَرَف). This is the night of great honor. Why? Consider what comes together: the most honorable of angels (Jibreel AS) came in this night, carrying the most honorable book of Allah ﷻ (the Quran), giving it to the most honorable messenger of Allah ﷻ (Muhammad ﷺ), in the most honored of nights. It's a convergence of all these glories and honors coming together in Laylat al-Qadr. The first ayah—إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ—is describing one of the things that makes this night so noble and honorable.

**Third meaning: Power**

Qadr also means power or ability. This is a night in which we should have complete faith in the power of Allah ﷻ to change things—to change our fate, to transform our behavior, to empower us to accomplish what we couldn't before. Allah ﷻ has endowed this night with unusual strength and power. After all, the greatest miracle ever given to humanity—the Quran—was given in this night. The most powerful Word of Allah ﷻ descended in this night. We should therefore have more belief in the miraculous answering of prayers this night than any other night of the year.

**A rare fourth meaning: Congestion**

There's one more rare interpretation worth mentioning. The word qadr can also mean congestion or constraint—when something is tightly packed together. This meaning appears in the Quran when describing limited provision: "And whoever has his provision restricted" (وَمَن قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُ). Applied here, some scholars suggest that so many angels descend on this night that the earth becomes congested with them. There are hadith indicating that more angels descend this night than there are grains of sand on the earth. This suggests that angels who normally remain in the heavens come down exclusively for this night. The world becomes so flooded with angels that there's no room left for any evil. It's completely filled with divine presence.

Among these interpretations, the most commonly emphasized—particularly in light of Surah Ad-Dukhan—is that this is the night of decree, when decisions are made for the coming year. And the greatest decision Allah ﷻ made for humanity—the coming of the final revelation—is also part of this night.

The scholars have called this the "anniversary of the Quran"—the night we celebrate the coming of Allah's final message. This should be considered the Eid of the Quran.

Before moving forward, we should address an important question: Why didn't Allah ﷻ tell us which specific night it is? There are narrations where the Prophet ﷺ remembered which night it was but was made to forget. 

Imam ar-Razi and others offer profound wisdom here. The Prophet ﷺ once told someone else to wake a sleeping person for prayer instead of doing it himself. When asked why, he explained: "If I did it and he lazily refused or dismissed me, that would be an act of disbelief—defying the Messenger. But if you do it and he dismisses you, he's just being lazy."

Apply this wisdom to Laylat al-Qadr. If we knew with certainty that it was tonight, and we dismissed it or were "not in the mood," that would be a major crime. After an entire surah has been revealed about how monumental this night is, to then casually skip it would be a grave violation. The greater something is, the greater its violation becomes.

It's actually a mercy of Allah ﷻ that He didn't specify the exact night. He didn't open that door for us to commit such a crime. Instead, He made us humbly seek this night across multiple nights. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who seek this night with humility and find it.

translation: And what can make you know what is the Night of Decree
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ

"And what can make you know what the Night of Power is?" This is Allah's way of saying: "I'm about to tell you the main things you need to know about Laylat al-Qadr. I'm going to share with you the core concepts that you need to internalize so you truly value this night and never lose sight of its significance."

This rhetorical question creates anticipation. It's a teaching method—grabbing your attention before delivering crucial information. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: "You can't fully grasp what this night really means, but let Me tell you what you need to understand."

The structure of this surah is remarkable. After this question, every subsequent ayah provides a unique answer—a different dimension of qadr. If you look at the key words in each ayah, you'll see the organizational beauty:

- The first couple of ayahs: قَدْر (decree)
- The third ayah: خَيْرٌ (better/goodness)  
- The fourth ayah: أَمْرٍ (command/matter)
- The final ayah: سَلَـٰمٌ (peace)

Each of these connects to a different meaning of qadr: decree, value, command, and duration. The surah's architecture is perfectly designed to answer the question "What is Laylat al-Qadr?" from multiple angles.

translation: The Night of Decree is better than a thousand months
commentary:
لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ

"The Night of Power is better than a thousand months." This is the first value Allah ﷻ assigns to this night—and remember, qadr also means value, so this is the night of value answering: What is its value?

Now, a thousand months calculated literally equals eighty-three years and four months. Some have taken this to mean that if you pray on this night, it's as if you prayed for 83 years. But this doesn't mean you can pray tonight and then take an 83-year vacation from prayer. That's not how this works.

When Allah ﷻ mentions large numbers like "a thousand" in the Quran—like "a thousand years of what you count" (أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ)—He's often indicating something beyond precise calculation. A thousand months essentially represents a full human lifetime. Eighty-some years is the average lifespan of a person.

So Allah's way of saying this is: In your estimation, this night is better than an entire lifetime. If you find this night and take advantage of it, you've found an entire good life for yourself.

Think about the layers of meaning here. We were just discussing the decisions that will unfold from this year to the next. But Allah ﷻ goes much further: This night is so noble, so grand, so powerful that it can set the course for the rest of your life. It can actually change everything about your existence. Laylat al-Qadr isn't just about the coming year—it can transform your entire future.

And notice: Allah ﷻ says خَيْرٌ مِّنْ—"better than." He didn't specify how much better. Is it a thousand times better than a thousand months? A million times better? He left it open, unqualified. He simply said it's better than a thousand months. The thousand months is just what we can imagine, but the reality is far beyond even that.

One more note on language: qadr in Arabic also means "value," so when Allah ﷻ asks "What is Laylat al-Qadr?" one meaning is "What is the night of value?" And the answer comes: Its value is more than a thousand months.

translation: The angels and the Spirit descend therein by permission of their Lord for every matter
commentary:
تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ

"The angels and the Spirit descend in it by permission of their Lord for every matter." After addressing the value of this night, Allah ﷻ now addresses another meaning of qadr: decree. This ayah deals with the commands of Allah ﷻ that come for every issue.

تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ—"The angels descend." All the angels come down from their stations in the heavens. And specifically mentioned: وَٱلرُّوحُ—"and the Spirit." This is Jibreel (AS), whom the Bible calls the Holy Spirit and the Quran calls Ar-Ruh. The angel who brought the Quran in the first place is now a special guest descending again.

Think about what we're discussing here. The original star of revelation—the one who brought the Quran to begin with—celebrates the anniversary of the Night of the Quran by coming down again himself. And he's described in the Quran as ذُو قُوَّةٍ—one of tremendous power, with a huge following. Not only Jibreel (AS), but his entire team that used to accompany him when bringing the Quran—all of them now descend together into the heavens and onto the earth.

This is the same Jibreel (AS) who brought revelation to Musa (AS), to Isa (AS), to all the prophets. And tonight he's in our company—he and all his followers, along with all the other angels.

You can think of angels as having different departments. The first heaven has its angels, the second has its angels, each assigned to different tasks. Allah ﷻ describes angels who guard us, angels who record our deeds, angels who continuously glorify Him around the Throne. Angels are doing different things throughout creation.

But tonight? All the angels have been taken off their regular tasks and sent down. Even Jibreel (AS) descends from the highest station—from Sidrat al-Muntaha, from the presence of divine revelation itself—he comes down for this night.

The phrase بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم—"by permission of their Lord"—is significant. Angels have their own work to do, their own assigned tasks. Allah ﷻ has given them freedom from whatever they normally do to come here, to descend for this purpose. That's why "by His permission" matters—this is a special dispensation, a unique night when the normal order is suspended for something greater.

مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ—"for every matter." They come with every kind of command, every kind of issue. Whether it's a health issue, a personal matter, a family concern, a matter of war or peace, a spiritual struggle, an emotional difficulty, a physical ailment, a financial problem—it doesn't matter what kind of issue. Allah ﷻ has decreed that the angels come to address all kinds of issues and help people with all matters. They're declaring the decree of Allah ﷻ, executing His commands for those who seek Him in this night.

The angels are all around us, hovering around us. They've flooded our space. They're ready to deliver what Allah ﷻ has written for us.

One crucial grammatical point: Notice Allah ﷻ uses the present tense—تَنَزَّلُ (they descend)—not the past tense. This suggests this isn't just describing that first time when the Quran was revealed. It keeps happening. This descent of the angels occurs every single year. It's not a one-time historical event. It's a gift Allah ﷻ gives to humanity annually, every Ramadan, every Laylat al-Qadr.

The scholars note that legions of angels descend, and among them the most noble. This is the gift of Allah ﷻ to the believers. Some commentators say the angels secure the commands of Allah ﷻ—they ensure His decree is executed properly.

translation: Peace it is until the emergence of dawn
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ

"Peace it is until the emergence of dawn." As we arrive at this final ayah, Allah ﷻ gives us yet another meaning of qadr. Remember, qadr can mean "limit" or "measure"—as in the ayah "Allah has set a measure for everything" (قَدْ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدْرًا). 

So beautifully organized: The first answer after the question "What is Laylat al-Qadr?" was about the value of this night (better than a thousand months). The second was about the decree of this night (angels descending with every command). And the third is about the limit or duration of this night—how long does it last?

And to me, this is the most fascinating aspect. Allah ﷻ says this night extends حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ—"until the emergence of dawn." Notice He didn't say until Fajr time begins, until the adhan is called, or until the first light appears on the horizon. He said until مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ—until the rising of dawn, when you can actually see the sunlight beginning to emerge, when the sky has turned and daylight is clearly coming.

This is remarkable. You know how our fast ends when it's still relatively dark, at Maghrib? The sun has set but darkness remains. Similarly, Fajr begins when the first light appears but it's still mostly dark. But Laylat al-Qadr? Allah ﷻ extended it. He made it continue until you start seeing actual light itself—until the dawn is emerging and rising.

He extended its value beyond the normal bounds of night. This is a unique gift of Laylat al-Qadr—Allah ﷻ enhanced its duration for us. Unlike any other night, He counts the early morning part—even as light begins to appear—as still part of this blessed night. That's an extra gift, extra time for worship, extra time for mercy.

Now let's examine the profound word سَلَـٰمٌ—peace. This is actually a very weighty word that dominates this surah. If you look at the key words throughout: there's qadr (power/decree), khayr (goodness), amr (command), and finally salam (peace). All of them connect to the central theme.

Those familiar with even basic Arabic will notice something unusual here. The normal sentence structure would be هِىَ سَلَـٰمٌ—"It is peace." But Allah ﷻ reversed the sequence: سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ—"Peace it is." This reversal creates tremendous emphasis. It's saying: This night—there is no peace like the peace of this night. There's no night that has the kind of peace this night has. Peace like no other exists in it.

Before we go deeper into salam, I should mention something endearing that some scholars have observed—though others dismiss it as overly creative. Some people hold the view that Laylat al-Qadr is specifically the 27th night. They literally counted the letters: the first word إِنَّآ has letters, the second word أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ has letters, continuing until when you reach هِىَ, that's the 27th letter in the surah. So they concluded: This is it—the 27th night.

Another numerical observation, also attributed to Ibn Abbas (RA): The words لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ consist of nine letters. These words appear three times in the surah. Nine times three equals 27—therefore the 27th night.

Some scholars say this is silly, that it has nothing to do with textual interpretation. But I mention it because it shows the depth of love and attention believers have given to studying this surah, searching for hints about this blessed night.

Now let's truly understand سَلَـٰمٌ, because this is a tremendously significant word in Islamic theology.

Salam is one of the names of Allah ﷻ Himself: ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ—"The King, the Pure, the Peace" (59:23). Similarly, Jannah is called Dar as-Salam—the Abode of Peace, the Place of Peace. So peace is a name of Allah ﷻ and the name for Paradise itself.

But salam also appears in contexts of danger being removed. When Ibrahim (AS) was thrown into the fire, Allah ﷻ commanded: كُونِى بَرْدًا وَسَلَـٰمًا—"Be coolness and safety" (21:69). Become peace itself, eliminate the danger completely.

Now notice the remarkable subtlety in this ayah. Allah ﷻ didn't say the night is سَالِمَة (peaceful)—He said سَلَـٰمٌ (peace itself). Grammatically, a night isn't literally peace—a night is peaceful. There's a difference. When we say "the garden is peaceful," we're using an adjective. But Allah ﷻ used an infinitive, an abstract noun: سَلَـٰمٌ—peace itself.

What does this mean? Not only is this night peaceful, but if you can truly take advantage of this night, you have found peace itself for the rest of your life. You've found ultimate, complete peace. It's as if you haven't truly experienced what peace means until you've found this night. That's the magnitude of the statement being made.

There's another dimension to consider. Salam can also function as a command. In English, a teacher might say "Silence!" instead of "Be silent!" A judge says "Order!" rather than "Be orderly!" Sometimes a noun itself issues a command.

Applied here, سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ becomes: Peace!—meaning you better be at peace. No fighting, no arguing, no conflicts. Allah ﷻ has sent the angels down, sent His decree down. This is the most valuable night of the year. You better be in complete submission before Allah ﷻ. You better be at peace with Him. 

Salam is the opposite of war, of conflict. You better not be argumentative with anyone. You better not be harsh or snippy with others. Be good with Allah ﷻ and good to humanity. Be at peace and in submission this night. It's like a divine suggestion—or really, a command—at the end: Until Fajr comes, drop every conflict, release every tension, let everything go. Just be at peace. سَلَـٰمٌ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ.

Others have drawn from this ayah guidance about recognizing Laylat al-Qadr: You'll feel a special kind of peace in this night that you don't feel otherwise. When you stand in prayer, you'll experience a tranquility that makes you want to remain in prayer—a feeling you've never had before. It's an unusual spiritual experience, the kind of calm Allah ﷻ gives a believer uniquely in this night. These are signs we look for in seeking Laylat al-Qadr.

---

**Signs of Laylat al-Qadr from Hadith Literature**

Let me share what we find in the hadith about seeking this night and recognizing it:

The Prophet ﷺ taught us that our lifespans, our provisions, everything about our coming year is decreed in this night. Angels descend with all that is good, with all blessings, with Allah's complete mercy and forgiveness.

As mentioned, the angels that descend may be ones who only come for this specific night—they don't descend at any other time. This is especially true of Jibreel (AS).

In one narration, the Prophet ﷺ said: "I was shown Laylat al-Qadr, then I was made to forget it. But it is in the last ten nights." He described it as a calm night—not too hot, not too cold, with a brilliant moon and stars. Shaytan doesn't emerge in it, and there's peace until the morning.

People are unusually at peace this night. Even when you go to the masjid, there's a different atmosphere—no one's annoyed, no pushing and shoving, children aren't causing chaos outside. Everything is calmer than usual. Something is different.

Some narrations mention the breeze is soft, and the rising sun has a gentle, reddish tone—it's not intense when it appears.

Allah ﷻ may show a believer which night it is in their sleep, as happened with several companions (RA).

The Prophet ﷺ said: "The number of angels on this night exceeds the number of pebbles and grains of sand on the earth." The earth is flooded with divine presence.

**How to Worship in Laylat al-Qadr**

Finally, the Prophet ﷺ taught us the greatest practice for this night: "Whoever stands in prayer during Laylat al-Qadr with faith and seeking reward, all their previous sins will be forgiven."

Let's understand the qualities of this prayer:

**Imanan**—out of faith. Pray driven by complete conviction that this prayer will be the best thing that's happened to you. Have confidence and reliance on Allah ﷻ in these prayers like no other time.

**Wa-htisaban**—seeking reward, taking full account. Be aware of your sins, your flaws, what you've done, what you're ashamed of before Allah ﷻ. Come before Allah ﷻ standing in prayer, ashamed and humiliated, yet completely convinced that He will forgive you, bless you, and let your sins pass. This is a confessional prayer—coming before Allah ﷻ humiliated, in tears, in full awareness of yourself.

If you can offer that kind of prayer—full of faith, full of self-awareness, full of hope in Allah's mercy—then every embarrassing sin, every mistake you've made, is guaranteed to be forgiven. All of it.

This kind of prayer is harder to do in congregation. It's much easier when you're by yourself, alone with Allah ﷻ. If you're someone who struggles to pray alone, then yes, come to the masjid and join the congregation. But this is the night where ideally you want it to be just you and Allah ﷻ, in tears, reciting Quran, making your supplications.

Become among those fortunate enough to have found this Laylat al-Qadr and found the forgiveness of Allah ﷻ in it.

We're also fortunate that our nights aren't very long anymore. There's not that much time between Isha and Fajr. Get some rest. Pray. Rest again. Pray again. Rest. Pray. Alternate like this across these nights.

May Allah ﷻ help us all find Laylat al-Qadr. May He give us familiarity with His Book and grant us the tears, sincerity, and strength to truly worship Him in these blessed nights.
